title: A scientific benefit (and cost) of AI innovation - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Tyler Cowen quotes researcher Carlo Cordasco's first-person account of how cheap AI-assisted preliminary exploration reshaped his research practice: faster idea-testing kills sunk-cost attachment and improves his 'question-identification' skill, but at the cost of eroded ability to hold a complex argument together verbally under pressure.

themes.theme: Benefit: cheaper exploration, better idea portfolio

insights.claim: AI collapsed the cost of preliminary research exploration for Carlo Cordasco, letting him sketch an argument, identify the first serious objections, test whether they were fatal, and reach a provisional verdict in an afternoon instead of a fortnight.
insights.speaker: Carlo Cordasco
insights.so_what: A concrete productivity multiple (roughly fortnight-to-afternoon) for AI-assisted research triage -- a useful anchor when modeling AI-driven productivity gains in knowledge work.
insights.quote: I could sketch an argument, identify the first serious objections, test whether they were fatal, and reach a provisional verdict in an afternoon rather than a fortnight.
insights.locator: What changed was that the cost of preliminary exploration collapsed

insights.claim: Because low exploration costs remove sunk-cost attachment, Cordasco now drops bad research questions earlier and more often, leaving him with a larger but better-curated portfolio of ideas to pursue.
insights.speaker: Carlo Cordasco
insights.so_what: Illustrates a second-order mechanism of AI productivity gains beyond raw speed -- better filtering/selection of which projects to pursue, applicable to any research- or idea-generation-heavy function.
insights.quote: the sunk cost attachment disappears, and you find yourself dropping bad questions earlier and more often, which means the questions you keep are better
insights.locator: When the exploration costs are low

insights.claim: The skill Cordasco says improved most from AI use is 'question-identification' -- the ability to find problems that are both tractable and important -- which he calls a core, historically untaught skill that academic careers are substantially built on.
insights.speaker: Carlo Cordasco
insights.so_what: Suggests AI's biggest edge in knowledge work may be augmenting problem selection rather than problem solving -- a differentiator to watch for in AI-research-tool products aimed at scientists and analysts.
insights.quote: something I can only describe as question-identification – the ability to find problems that are both tractable and important

insights.claim: Cordasco believes his ability to hold together a complex position verbally, under pressure, in a seminar or conversation, has probably not improved and may have declined, because cheap AI-assisted exploration reduces the first-principles grinding that builds live argumentative fluency.
insights.speaker: Carlo Cordasco
insights.so_what: A candid cost case against uncritical AI productivity narratives -- flags a skill-atrophy risk (live verbal reasoning) relevant to how AI-native cohorts will perform in real-time debate, negotiation, or advisory settings.
insights.quote: My ability to hold together a complex position verbally, under pressure, in a seminar or a conversation, has probably not improved and may have declined somewhat.
insights.locator: I want to be honest about the costs
